game_name: Leisure Suit Larry in the Land of the Lounge Lizards

danger_signals.visual_form: Character death animation; compartment opening beneath Larry's body (original) or blender sequence (1991 remake)
danger_signals.meaning: Failure state - player has made irreversible mistake or time expired

danger_signals.visual_form: Character status change after unprotected intercourse with sex worker
danger_signals.meaning: Health danger - immediate death follows if not prevented; visual consequence of risky action

danger_signals.visual_form: Real-time countdown (7 hours original, 8 hours remake); visual clock or status indicator
danger_signals.meaning: Existential danger - Larry commits suicide at time limit; world-state of despair

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental danger in non-adjacent areas; triggered when attempting travel without taxi
danger_signals.meaning: Economic/physical danger - loss of resources or death if trying to traverse city improperly

danger_signals.visual_form: Low money counter; Larry starts with only $94
danger_signals.meaning: Resource scarcity - inability to access taxi travel, gambling, or purchase items leads to failure

safety_signals.visual_form: Women respond favorably after receiving appropriate gifts; detailed portrait imagery shows changed expression/attitude
safety_signals.meaning: Progress indicator - relationship advancement toward final goal; world-state improvement

safety_signals.visual_form: Item obtained at convenience store; inventory state change
safety_signals.meaning: Safety mechanism - prevents STD death; enables continuation past initial encounter

safety_signals.visual_form: Blackjack and slot machine gameplay; money counter increase
safety_signals.meaning: Resource generation - acquiring funds needed for taxi travel and item purchases

safety_signals.visual_form: Player can walk freely between neighboring locations (Lefty's Bar, casino, chapel, disco, convenience store)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe traversal - no resource cost or danger for movement within connected zones

safety_signals.visual_form: Key women (Fawn, Faith, Eve) shown in detailed character portraits during interaction, unlike generic NPCs
safety_signals.meaning: Narrative importance - visual elevation indicates these are quest-critical relationships

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four distinct women: sex worker (seedy-looking), Fawn (club-goer), Faith (receptionist), Eve (bathing beauty). Non-key women appear as generic NPCs without detailed portraits.
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Located in specific establishments - Faith in hotel reception, Eve as bathing beauty (pool/resort area implied), Fawn in disco/club, sex worker accessible from start location

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Neutral NPCs (bartenders, shopkeepers, etc.) - non-hostile but transactional
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Lefty's Bar, hotel-casino, 24-hour wedding chapel, disco, convenience store - each location represents different commercial/social opportunity

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Characters from King's Quest and other Sierra games appear in death laboratory/compartment scene
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Laboratory beneath starting location; activated only on character death - represents meta-game failure realm

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Larry's 1970s leisure suit (out-of-style); minimal starting money ($94 visible); monochrome or limited EGA palette graphics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Women shun Larry initially; must acquire gifts to advance; forced to gamble to increase resources

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Real-time countdown displayed; visual urgency increases as 7-8 hour limit approaches
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player movement becomes more frantic; NPCs may show different availability based on time-of-day (hotel night operations, disco active hours)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Key women's detailed portraits change expression/stance based on gift-giving success; visual intimacy increases in portrait display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Women transition from rejection to favorable response; access to final penthouse area unlocked via accumulated relationship progress

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Condom item in inventory; time limit removed from gameplay UI after safe first encounter
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game transitions from survival-mode (7-hour clock) to open-ended quest mode; reduced immediate danger state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Money counter critically low or zero; inaccessible taxi UI or warning messages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Forced gambling to continue; mugging or traffic death threats for attempting unpaid travel between non-adjacent areas

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Clock reaches zero; character animation shifts to suicide pose/action
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game Over state triggered; automatic restart sequence initiated (death compartment/blender scene in 1991 remake shows humorous death animation)

environmental_storytelling.example: Lefty's Bar, casino, wedding chapel, disco, convenience store - each represents different social/economic opportunity in resort city; Lost Wages as parody of Las Vegas communicates hedonistic setting through venue selection
Key romance targets receive detailed character artwork; generic NPCs lack portraits - visual distinction telegraphs quest importance without explicit UI labels

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: Real-time 7-8 hour countdown and ultimate suicide consequence - communicates world-state of desperation and Larry's existential crisis through mechanical constraint

environmental_storytelling.example: Starting with only $94 and out-of-style leisure suit - visual economy reflects Larry's 'loser' status; money counter becomes primary narrative tension
Sierra game character appearances in death laboratory/blender scene - breaks fourth wall to communicate game's own development history and studio identity through failure state
Larry's campy 1970s leisure suit persists visually throughout game - costume communicates personality, era disconnect, and perpetual 'loser' identity regardless of quest progress

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Accepts any available romantic opportunity; questions moral validity but proceeds when desperate; attempts risky unprotected encounter without full information
Shun Larry; refuse interaction until gift-giving establishes value; show favorable response only after receiving appropriate gifts
Accessible immediately from game start; willing to engage without gifts; communicates STD risk through game mechanics rather than visual warning
Required for travel between non-adjacent areas; costs money; failure to pay results in mugging or traffic death - behavioral consequence for poor resource management
Only method to increase starting capital; blackjack and slots accessible to desperate player; visual money counter changes based on wins/losses
Countdown from 7-8 hours; at zero, Larry commits suicide automatically; no UI message required - mechanical consequence communicates despair
Provide services without detailed character art; minimal dialogue; represent world infrastructure rather than narrative focus

notes: Leisure Suit Larry 1 uses visual-mechanical integration to communicate world-state rather than explicit narrative exposition. The 1970s leisure suit persists as constant visual identifier of Larry's 'loser' status. Danger signals operate primarily through mechanical consequence (death, STD, time limit) rather than explicit visual warnings. The game establishes visual hierarchy through character portrait presence/absence to indicate quest relevance. Death sequences feature meta-narrative breaks (Sierra game character cameos) that communicate both failure state and developer identity. The real-time 7-8 hour countdown is the primary world-state manifestation, creating existential urgency communicated through clock mechanic rather than environmental visual deterioration. Relationship progression tracked through portrait expression changes rather than environmental or dialogue cues. Economic scarcity drives all behavioral changes - world-state of poverty is both mechanically enforced and visually represented through starting condition (low money counter, out-of-style attire).
